Enigmatig Is Producing Contracting Revenue And Operating Profit

EGG is seeking US public investor capital to fund its various operations and growth plans.

The firm’s financials have generated dropping topline revenue from a small base, lower gross profit and gross margin, reduced operating profit, but higher cash used in operations.

Free cash flow for the twelve months ended Sept. 30, 2024, was negative ($606,109).

The company's growth strategy entails expanding into new jurisdictions, broadening its service offerings and reducing pricing to gain market share.

Payroll & Employee Benefits expenses as a percentage of total revenue have fallen as topline revenue has dropped, a negative result; its Payroll & Employee Benefits efficiency multiple was negative (1.5x) in the most recent fiscal year.

The firm currently intends to pay zero dividends and to retain future earnings, if any, for its working capital and growth requirements.

EGG’s recent capital spending results show it has continued to spend on capital expenditures despite generating negative operating cash flow.

The market opportunity for regulatory compliance work in the Asia-Pacific region is moderate in size and expected to grow in the coming years.

However, the industry is increasingly fragmented and subject to intensely competitive pricing pressures, which may already be hampering EGG’s growth and profitability.

The company will have risks as a public entity, including its "emerging growth company" and "foreign private issuer" status, which enables management to divulge substantially less information to shareholders.

Also, many Asia-based companies have typically had minimal communication with shareholders.

Management is seeking an EV/revenue multiple of approximately 29x on declining topline revenue, an excessive valuation expectation.

Given the firm’s declining financial results, exuberant valuation assumptions and numerous risks, my outlook on the EGG IPO is to sell (avoid it).

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced.

data.title: Crypto Market Update: Bitcoin's Relative Strength Amid U.S. Equities Decline
data.description:
Summary

What an eventful week in the markets… Futures markets gapped higher on the Sunday open on March 23, 2025, and had a strong start to the week. The S&P 500 (SP500) traded up over 117 handles from the prior Friday’s close, the NASDAQ Composite Index (COMP:IND) climbed over 554 handles, and the Dow Jones Industrial Average Index (DJI) gained more than 800 handles before pulling back.

If you take a look at the chart posted above, it's obvious that the markets gave back all the weekly gains, reversed, and closed aggressively bearish for the week.

In terms of market structure, both the daily and weekly charts have formed a lower high after a few days of upside following the formation of a base. This indicates to me that U.S. equities most likely have further downside ahead.

As mentioned in an earlier write-up of mine, the key level I was watching on the Nasdaq (21,869.50) acted as resistance and ultimately marked the formation of the monthly local top.

Now that we've covered what the stock indices did this past week, I want to discuss how the crypto market has reacted to the broader macro downside and highlight how Bitcoin USD (BTC-USD) is actually showing some relative strength amid this decline.

Bitcoins Relative Strength

No matter your stance on crypto or Bitcoin itself, this market is currently showing strength amid the recession-driven downside in U.S. equities.

At the time of writing, Bitcoin is down 25% from its all-time high of $110,000 during the presidential inauguration, currently trading at $82,200.

The last time the S&P 500 was down around 10%, Bitcoin had declined by over 50% and continued to fall as US Equities fell.

Note: An important point to note is that Bitcoin is not immune to a stock market sell-off, as we've clearly seen over the last few weeks and previous years. However, the key difference this time is that Bitcoin is showing relative strength compared to the stock market, which is different from the last 'major' market sell-off in 2022.

When comparing the recent lows printed on both the S&P 500 and Bitcoin, Bitcoin made its HTF low on March 11th, 2025, while the S&P made a lower low and its HTF low on March 13th, 2025.

This does not mean that Bitcoin or the S&P have bottomed. As mentioned in the summary, I believe we most likely have more downside before any major upside in U.S. equities. However, the key point here is that, based on recent data, Bitcoin is showing strength compared to U.S. equities, which usually isn’t the case.

What Does This Strength Mean?

There are a few scenarios that, I think, could play out for Bitcoin:

1. First, the overall markets continue to sell off as we've seen, and Bitcoin follows, but it holds up much better than the declines we've seen in the past.

2. Second, the overall markets enter a consolidation phase where there aren't many new headlines, earnings for large companies remain solid, and they hold up well due to new innovations (e.g., AI). In this scenario, Bitcoin could rally without equities pulling the market down.

3. Third, which I'm not sure is likely but is important to consider, is that Bitcoin begins to detach itself from the stock market as fear increases. The recent trend unfolding is that liquidity is leaving the U.S. markets (U.S. equities, stocks, bonds). In my opinion, there is a scenario where Bitcoin could see increased inflows as it becomes viewed as a universal asset for other countries with weaker currencies to flock to if this U.S. downturn begins to hurt everyone.

4. Lastly, I could simply be wrong about Bitcoin's strength, and we may see another major leg lower towards the 50k-60k range. (This is where risk management comes into play)

Risk To Thesis

When I develop trade ideas and form a thesis to potentially act on, there is always the possibility of it being wrong. For example, in my recent article on Ethereum USD (ETH-USD), I took a long entry that was playing out well, as I was up over 10% on that trade over a few weeks. However, as equities rolled over this past week, so did crypto, especially altcoins.

In that Ethereum analysis, I posted frequent updates, noting that I took small partials at $2,100 and managed risk by moving my stop loss to a level I was comfortable with. If the price traded back down to that level, I would want to exit the trade.

Since then, I have been stopped out in profit. This is why risk management is crucial, understanding potential downside risks helps manage expectations.

When it comes to the risks of the strength and upside of Bitcoin, there are a few I would monitor.

US Equities

One is the simple price movement in US equities. In the last few years, as Bitcoin has gained more adoption, it has primarily moved with the stock market. You've had times, though, when Bitcoin showed weakness and traded lower or exhibited strength and traded higher, even as the stock market was trending in either direction (e.g., the FTX collapse, China regulations, Tesla discontinuing Bitcoin payments, and China's support for blockchain in 2019, which caused a major rally as the S&P fell).

But is that the case currently? No.

Currently, we are seeing a macro trend in which all risk-on markets are taking a hit, regardless of asset class. Past data shows that Bitcoin tends to follow this trend, as it did in 2018, 2020, and 2022. With that said, watching the stock market, especially the indices, will be crucial, as I see it as one of the main risks to Bitcoin at the moment. Sentiment is a price driver in the Bitcoin market.

Technical Levels

Next, I would monitor key technical levels on the charts. If we start breaking below the HTF structure, which I still find somewhat bullish on the weekly and monthly charts, it would pose a further risk to the strength that has been discussed.

I have three key levels I'm watching on Bitcoin that still give me a bullish bias on the HTF chart.

The first level is the 50% retracement of the weekly trading range, which is around $76,000–$78,000.

The second level is the previous all-time high, around $73,800.

The last level is $66,810, which marks the start of the rally we saw when Trump was elected. This rally occurred across all markets, but crypto—mainly Bitcoin—saw a significant injection of liquidity. What's also interesting is that the S&P is currently trading below the low of the Trump rally, which again confirms the relative strength of Bitcoin.

If Bitcoin starts to trade back down towards these key price levels, which have a narrative behind them, I would want to see clear demand. If the price does not show signs of demand, that would pose a clear risk to the strength thesis here.

The best-case scenario is that the absolute bottom for Bitcoin was when we retested the 50% retracement, which is a possibility. However, I would like to see the daily structure turn more bullish.

Conclusion

I believe this is not the time to be deeply bearish on Bitcoin, or at least to sell "everything" and run for the hills, as Bitcoin is actually showing decent strength during this downturn.

The question is: Do we see a continued heavy sell-off in US equities, or do things stabilize and consolidate, allowing Bitcoin the room to make a run? That’s what I’m currently watching and what I think may potentially play out in the near term.

data.title: We Are Buying REIT Preferred Stocks Part 2: Summit Hotel Or Sunstone Hotel Preferreds?
data.description:
For a long time, we avoided investing in preferred stocks due to their narrow spreads. However, the spreads have recently become more reasonable, which has made us reconsider investing in REIT preferred stocks. We find them much more attractive now, primarily since we've focused on the safest options first, particularly those offering yields above 6.5%. That's why we decided to start this series and introduce you to these stocks.

Today, we will talk about Summit Hotel Properties (NYSE:INN) and try to compare it with Sunstone Hotel Investors (NYSE:SHO). Both of these REITs share many similarities, such as their structure, yields, revenues, and more. In today's article, we’ll analyze which one presents a better investment opportunity. Without further delay, let's begin.

Company Overviews

Nothing extraordinary here. Both REITs are operating in the lodging business and are trying to offer an experience that "exceeds expectations."

They're also well diversified in different regions across the country, or at least that's what they claim. But as much as we would like to talk about future vacation plans, it's best that we turn our attention to the basic financial metrics.

At the time of writing, INN has approximately $2.9 billion of assets on the balance sheet, whereas SHO has close to $3.1 billion.

  • In terms of revenue, INN has generated approximately $730 million. yearly for the past few years, while SHO has generated roughly $900 million. They have a price-to-book value of 0.65 and 1.04, respectively. When we do the calculations, we end up with a market-adjusted asset yield of approximately 21.6% for the former and 20.52% for the latter. There are two main instruments that investors can purchase to be involved in the capital structure of the companies:

Common Stock

Both have seen some pressure lately. Below, we can see the basic valuation metrics:

SHO's forward FFO yield comes at 9.98%, whereas INN's forward FFO yield is 16.84%.

Preferred Stock

Below, you can see both companies' preferred stocks along with their metrics.

Only INN.PR.E is callable, whereas the rest are still behind their call date. You can see that all of their preferreds trade at similar prices and give similar yields. The main difference between them is their credibility. Let's see how we do it.

Credit Score

Please take a look at the picture below:

An important thing to note is that we use an adjusted version of Moody's approach to assessing financial strength within the REIT sector. Moody's considers several key factors, each with a different weight, when evaluating these companies:

I. Scale (5%):

  • Gross assets. Book value of assets.

II. Business Profile (25%):

  • Asset quality. If the assets are located in global gateway cities, and the quality of tenants.
  • Operating environment, geographic diversification, REIT market trends, and the sector the company operates.

III. Liquidity and Access to Capital (35%):

  • Liquidity and capital. Levels of liquidity and capital available to cover debt maturities.
  • Unencumbered assets/gross assets. The percentage of unencumbered assets.

IV. Leverage and Coverage (45%):

  • Total debt plus preferred stock /gross assets.
  • Net debt/EBITDA. Here, we take the total debt instead of the net debt to give a more pessimistic score.
  • Secured debt/gross assets.
  • Fixed-charge coverage. EBITDA /(interest and dividend payment on its preferred stock).

Disclosure: For "asset quality," "operating environment," and "liquidity," for the benefit of the doubt, we always try to give slightly worse scores. The reason for this can be seen in the pictures below:

In short, Moody's generally believes that the hotel industry bears more risk since they have less liquidity and cyclical in nature. Both SHO and INN were given pessimistic scores for "asset quality," "operating environment" and "liquidity" because of the sole fact that they operate in the lodging business.

That being said, in our evaluations, you can both see the balance sheet score and the overall score, which includes the more subjective parts. We also give an adjusted credit score based on the current market valuation of the company.

Now that we have cleared out how we do the scorings, let's turn our attention to INN and SHO and use the formula above to assign them a credit score.

SHO and INN Metrics and Comparison

These are INN's metrics of interest. INN gets a final balance sheet score of 8.15, and when we adjust it according to the mentioned earlier P/B value, we end up getting a score of 8.42, a Baa1 equivalent.

Moving on to SHO:

SHO's overall balance sheet score is 5.61, an A2 equivalent, but the public has assessed the score to be actually closer to 5.58, which is, again, an A2 equivalent.

Like we mentioned earlier, both companies have preferreds that offer similar returns. You can see them in the chart below (we calculated a hypothetical duration so that we can show them on the chart).

So, it turns out that when we invest in INN's preferreds, we end up getting similar yields to those that SHO offers while carrying an approximate three-notch higher credit risk equivalent.

Statistical Comparison

Historically, SHO preferred stocks are also in buy territory based on their regression model, which is just an addition to the fundamental logic.

The Bottom Line - Buy SHO, Sell INN

SHO has approximately 100% unencumbered assets, whereas INN has close to 97%.

SHO has a total debt + preferreds / gross assets of approximately 26%, while INN has close to 43%.

SHO's Debt/ EBITDA is close to 55, while INN's Debt/EBITDA is roughly 7

SHO has no secured debt. While INN's secured debt is close to 2% of the assets.

Lastly, SHO's fixed-charge coverage ratio is 3.54, while INN's is 2.33

Both REITs are similar in structure and offer preferreds with similar yields. The main difference between them is their credit scoring, and we believe SHO to be the clear winner here.

data.title: Franklin Electric: A Fascinating Business That Doesn't Warrant Much Optimism
data.description:
One interesting company that I have been eyeballing for quite some time is Franklin Electric Co., Inc. (NASDAQ:FELE). The firm dates back to 1944, and is currently incorporated in the great state of Indiana. With a market capitalization of $4.47 billion as of this writing, it is a pretty sizable business. And during its life, it has grown to focus on a very interesting market. This is none other than the water industry.

The fact of the matter is that water is a truly precious resource on this planet. Climate change and growing populations continue to create challenges when it comes to this commodity. And in order to meet those challenges, a company like Franklin Electric is essential.

Over the last few years, financial results for the business have been mixed but generally rangebound. This is disappointing in and of itself. Even so, I could be bullish on this firm if shares were trading at low enough levels. Sadly, that does not appear to be the case. While the stock is cheap compared to other similar enterprises, it stands quite solidly in the fair value range as I see it. Because of this, I do believe that it's best to keep the firm rated a "hold" for now. But if we do see strong financial performance at some point in the future, or if shares drop quite a bit from where they are, it could be an interesting candidate for value investors like myself.

An interesting business

According to the management team at Franklin Electric, the company operates as a producer and seller of water pumping systems. These are systems that move both fresh water and wastewater for residential, agricultural, and certain industrial end markets. On top of this, the company sells groundwater equipment products to well installation contractors. These include water pumping systems that it makes available through both its own distribution locations and third-party branches that it works with throughout the US. Over the years, the firm has expanded its footprint, not only geographically, but also in terms of the products that it provides. As an example, it serves as a supplier of submersible fueling systems that are used at gas stations. It also makes pumps, pipes, electronic controls, and monitoring devices for this space.

The business has a massive operation, with a global footprint that spans 6 continents and 20 countries. Each day that it operates, it produces 20,000 pumps, motors, drives, and controls. This is all to cater to the water and fuel industries that permeate all aspects of society. According to management, around 3 trillion gallons of fresh water are used each day on this planet. 1 billion gallons of fuel is also used. Naturally, this would open up the door to some significant opportunities for the firm’s investors. Water systems, for instance, are estimated to be a global market opportunity of at least $12 billion. This is certainly the largest market that the company caters to.

However, there are two others. The firm’s Distribution segment Focuses on the US groundwater market. This is outside of the Water Systems market that prioritizes water pumping systems. This is a market opportunity that's worth over $4 billion. Meanwhile, the Energy Systems segment, which focuses on fuel pumping systems and related offerings, is worth over $1.3 billion.

As large as these market opportunities are, the recent financial performance achieved by the business has been a bit disappointing. In 2024, the company generated revenue of $2.02 billion. This was down from the $2.07 billion reported the previous year and was down from the $2.04 billion reported the year before that. Frankly, the decline has come from two of the three operating segments that the company has. Take the 2023 to 2024 fiscal years as an example.

During this time, the Water Systems segment for the company saw revenue drop from $1.20 billion to $1.18 billion. Management claimed that this was because of lower volumes caused by weaker end market demand for large dewatering equipment. It didn't help that foreign currency fluctuations also negatively impacted results. Year over year, there was also a decline for the Energy Systems segment, with revenue contracting from $296.5 million to $273.7 million. For context, in 2022, revenue was even higher at $334.1 million. Management didn't really provide much in the way of detail here, except to say that these declines were the result of lower volumes.

And finally, there is the Distribution segment. This has been the one bright spot for the company from a revenue perspective. From 2022 to 2023, revenue rose from $668.1 million to $673.3 million. There was a subsequent increase to $685.5 million last year. This growth seems to have been the result of acquisitions that the company has been making in this industry.

On the bottom line, the picture has been mixed as well. Between 2022 and 2024, net profits had been range bound between $180.3 million and $193.3 million. Other profitability metrics have been a bit more volatile. Operating cash flow, for instance, nearly tripled over the last three years from $101.7 million to $261.4 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we actually get a decline from $274.9 million to $257.9 million. The only consistent change during this three-year window has been with EBITDA. Over the last three fiscal years, it dropped from $318.9 million to $306.8 million.

For the 2025 fiscal year, management does seem a bit more optimistic. They expect revenue to be between $2.09 billion and $2.15 billion. At the midpoint, this would translate to an increase of 4.9% compared to what the company saw last year. Earnings per share should be between $4.05 and $4.25. At the midpoint, that would translate to net profits of $193 million. Seeing as how this has still been range-bound for the last few years, I don't think it would be terribly useful to project out what the cash flow metrics for the firm might be. These probably won't be materially different from net profits.

Using the historical data provided by management, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. Honestly, these are trading multiples that I would typically associate with a company that is in the fair value range. I almost always prioritize the absolute valuation of a company over its relative valuation. However, when stacked up against other similar enterprises, shares do look to be a bit on the cheap end of the spectrum. In the table below, you can see how it compares to five similar firms. On a price-to-earnings basis, it ended up being the cheapest of the group. But when we use the other two profitability metrics, one of the five firms ended up being cheaper than it is.

Company Price/Earnings Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
Franklin Electric Co., Inc. 24.8 17.3 14.3
ESCO Technologies Inc. (ESE) 37.6 27.1 20.3
Mueller Water Products, Inc. (MWA) 31.0 18.6 15.8
Kadant Inc. (KAI) 36.5 26.2 19.2
Gates Industrial Corporation plc (GTES) 27.1 13.8 10.5
Enpro Inc. (NPO) 50.3 22.6 17.7

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I do think that Franklin Electric is a fascinating business. But honestly, it's not attractive enough to me to justify a bullish assessment. It would be one thing if fundamental performance was improving rapidly. If that were the case, the trading multiples that we see might be justifiable. If the stock was cheaper instead, that could also make it appealing. Long term, I fully expect that the company will do well. But if your goal is to capture strong returns, it might be best to look elsewhere for opportunities. Ultimately, I believe that rating the company a "hold" is the best that I can do at this time.

data.title: Heads-Up
data.description:
Historically, when the S&P 500 (SPX, SP500) has fallen 10% and the yield on the 10-year U.S. Treasury bond (US10Y) has declined 50+ basis points, the U.S. economy has been heading into recession about 70% of the time. We reached both milestones in March. The economically sensitive small-cap Russell 2000 index (RTY) is down 17% since last November, and Canada’s resource and financial-centric TSX (SPTSX) is down 6% since December 6, 2024.

It’s worth noting that the average stock market decline during past recessions has been 30%, and 34-86% when valuations begin from extreme highs, as in 1929, 1973, 1999, 2007, 2020 (below) and now.

While official unemployment rates are still not far above cycle lows, credit delinquency rates (90+ days late) are already near their highest levels in 20 years.

The recent University of Michigan Sentiment Survey registered the greatest level of pessimism on personal finances ever recorded - worse than 2008. Just let that settle in a minute.

Knock-on risks intensify with age. As of early 2024, individuals 55+ held approximately 80% of all stocks, a significant increase from 60% two decades ago.

Everyone hates to see their portfolios decline, but capital loss later in life is harder to recover emotionally, psychologically, and financially. Losses while needing to withdraw for living expenses can severely reduce the longevity of savings and the likelihood that portfolios never fully recover - a scenario known as sequence of return risk. Outliving one’s savings remains among the top retirement fears.

As the economy and employment contract from here, central banks will continue easing efforts, but inflationary fears around tariffs may slow the pace and degree, at least in the near term.

For those who hope coming cuts are bullish for risk assets, it bears noting that the bulk of price declines have always come while the Fed is easing, not before.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Ceragon Networks: The Good And The Bad
data.description:
Introduction

The stock of Ceragon Networks Ltd. (NASDAQ:CRNT), an Israeli-based entity that renders end-to-end wireless transport solutions to clients (basically mobile network service providers and private networks) across 130 countries (although 43% of its annual revenue comes from the Indian market alone), has encountered quite a brutal start to 2025. On a YTD basis, when other international small-caps have eked out positive gains of around 3% (on average), CRNT has plummeted, losing roughly half its value.

So, should the current crash be viewed as an opportune time to pick up a quality stock on the cheap, or is it more sensible to stay on the sidelines and perhaps look elsewhere? Well, we feel quite conflicted about this story, but here are some positive and negative sub-plots that may help you make a call on CRNT. We’ll first start with the former before transitioning to the latter.

The Onset of IP-100E Puts Ceragon In An Enviable Place

While cellular networks across the world are still largely based on 4G infrastructure, the growing proliferation of enhanced mobile broadband, IoT (Internet of Things), gigabit broadband, enterprise-related multi-gigabit services, etc. are all serving as key catalysts in facilitating a transition towards 5G network infrastructure. It is estimated that approximately four years from now, around 85% of the global population will have access to a 5G network!

Against this backdrop, one shouldn’t underestimate the importance of top-tier wireless transport specialists such as Ceragon, that help cellular operators and the like, in enhancing their networks at relatively healthy economics. CRNT, we’d like to believe, is ahead of the competition as it has been tweaking and developing a highly efficient multicore technology for close to 17 years now. The most recent iteration of their multicore expertise comes in the form of an SoC-based (System-on-a-chip) e-band wireless networking solution called IP-100E, that looks very well-positioned to cater to the heightened backhaul demands associated with 5G networks at a relatively low cost of ownership.

With an E-band frequency range of 71-86 GHz and capacity of 25 Gbps in a single box (potentially even 40 Gbps under a multi-box set-up), more than any other product in the industry, the IP-100E looks very well set to handle large volumes of traffic generated by 5G, more so in an urban environment. Crucially, given that e-band licensing is not cost prohibitive across most countries, there could be plenty of takers. Note that, the salubrious effects of IP-100E won’t be felt immediately as it will only go into mass production in 2026, but it certainly enhances the future allure of CRNT.

Cash-Generation Poised To Step Up This Year

Then, those who follow investment principles that are deeply centered around the concept of cash-flow generation will be enthused to note that CRNT’s prospects are looking up in 2025, after a rather damp FY24 when FCF dropped by 44% YoY and came in at less than $12m. On the Q4 call management suggested that improved component availability is helping them reduce inventories, while we also think there’s scope for CRNT to get more efficient with its trade receivables (the largest component of their current assets, accounting for 57%) which is currently at 5-year highs. Better cash generation dynamics also help CRNT hold on to its net cash position (where cash on the books exceeds total financial debt) which it only attained in Q4 last year.

Note that even without the potential improvements in store, at the current market cap, CRNT’s FCF yield of almost 5% is better than its long-term average. One year from now, we could be looking at a much better yield as management is on record stating that “we also expect to increase our free cash flow compared to 2024 levels”.

Near-Term Revenue Outlook Looks Underwhelming Despite E2E Tech’s Contribution

Last year, CRNT’s revenue rose by 13%, and in Q4 alone, the revenue rose by a higher margin of 18%. If CRNT could keep up similar run rates within the double-digit percentage threshold, few stakeholders would fret, but the reality is that only half of last year’s revenue growth came from organic growth with the rest coming from the Silklu acquisition.

Note that the outlook ahead for the next four quarters looks rather underwhelming, with the topline expected to decline in H1-25. This is despite a $15m-$19m positive revenue contribution from the E2E Technologies acquisition. This should not come as a huge surprise as the wireless transport market (particularly for communication service providers) in general, is seeing weakness (for context the market declined by 10% last year) with limited forward visibility.

Heightened Indian Market Exposure May Not Necessarily Be Too Lucrative

As highlighted earlier, CRNT is strongly exposed to the Indian market, and it looks like its stake in this country is only growing with an all-time revenue record there in Q4 (42% exposure across the whole year, but 52% in Q4 alone). Another e-band based product of theirs- IP-50EX is seeing great traction in the Indian market there.

We have little doubt that CRNT’s expertise is very well-regarded by its Indian clients, and that should be heartening to note for a country with a relatively high 5G penetration runway (as of late 2023, 5G penetration was only in early double-digits).

However, reports coming out of Asia suggest that 5G deployment in India isn’t going as smoothly as one initially envisaged, with representatives of Indian telcos also pointing to some financial stress. CRNT has already noted in its 10-K that its sales to India are “generally at lower gross margins in comparison to other regions” so it is probably not in their best interest to price their products even more competitively in the Indian market.

Since CRNT is also in the midst of stepping up some new product launches, one can expect higher start-up costs associated with the same which will impact margins. As you can see from the image below, CRNT’s margins haven’t been trending too strongly in recent quarters, and after only coming in at 11.4% for Q4, CRNT is only hoping to hit operating margins of 10% for FY25, which is not particularly great.

That’s why we feel investors should not get too euphoric about CRNT’s dirt-cheap P/E multiple of just 8.35x (which is one of the cheapest amongst other communication equipment stocks), as that low multiple does not offer earnings growth but negative earnings growth of 31%.

Closing Thoughts - Bears Continue To Dominate Proceedings, Although There Could Be An Intermediate Recovery

There’s little doubt that CRNT's stock still largely appears to be in the control of the bears, as the stock is trading below all three of its key moving averages (50, 100 and 200DMA), with the 50DMA (red line) even dropping below the 100DMA (green line) for the first time since November 2024.

However, if you’re looking for some green shoots, one could also argue that the selling appears to have gone a bit overboard, with the stock now trading at its lower Bollinger band (the lower black line which represents two standard deviations away from the 20DMA). Also, note that the stock has now reached a price range which previously served as a congestion zone from September to November last year. We are not saying with any certainty that this will happen, but don’t be surprised to see history being repeated.

To get a broader perspective, one may also take some insight from the longer-term monthly chart. The two black lines (support and resistance) highlight the 12-year trading range of this stock. Within this broad trading range, we also have a 3-year trading range (highlighted in blue) which CRNT appeared to have broken out of in November last year. However, the risk of further upward momentum, beyond the 12-year resistance, was always going to be dicey, as we’ve seen previous instances (in 2018, 2019, 2021) of candles with long upper wicks, reflecting the additional supply of shares, and capping further gains.

Essentially, after a false breakout, CRNT has now dropped back to more comfortable levels within the 12-year range, where the risk-reward looks more favorable (the price is closer to support than resistance). Having said that, please also be aware that CRNT is still perched around 50% higher than its long-term support, so further downside risk should not be ruled out after the stock makes a potential intermediate recovery. Going with a HOLD on CRNT.

data.title: PDT: Improved Distribution Coverage And Healthy NAV Growth
data.description:
Overview

John Hancock Premium Dividend Fund (NYSE:PDT) operates as a closed-end fund that aims to generate attractive total returns from its blended portfolio of common, bonds, and preferred stocks. I previously covered PDT several quarters ago and issued a buy rating due to the positive outlook around interest rate cuts. Since my last coverage, management has released an updated annual report and the outlook around interest rate cuts has changed. So I wanted to take this as an opportunity to revisit the fund to provide some updated insights into the current performance, valuation, and dividend sustainability.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that PDT's price has increased by 11.85%. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 21.6% over the same period. One of the main appeals of PDT is the generous starting dividend yield of about 7.5%. The higher yield makes this an attractive income-producing asset for investors who are retired and are seeking a reliable source of supplemental income to fund lifestyle expenses. The fund has demonstrated a combination of capital preservation and a sufficient level of dividend consistency. Although dividend growth in recent years leaves room for improvement, I still believe PDT makes for a great income-producing asset.

Despite the recent price increase over the last year, PDT still offers an attractive value proposition. The latest annual report indicates that net assets have increased at a considerable rate compared to the prior year, as well as a healthy dividend coverage. Additionally, the price still trades at an attractive discount to NAV valuation. So let's start by taking a look at the fund's strategy and how it generates the necessary earnings to support the higher dividend yield.

Fund Strategy

According to the fund's latest breakdown, total managed assets amount to slightly over $1B and are spread across 124 different holdings. The fund does utilize leverage in an effort to amplify returns when an attractive opportunity presents itself. Leverage currently totals 35.52% of the fund's assets, which I consider to be quite aggressive given current macroeconomic conditions. While the utility of leverage is always with well-intent, leverage can also work against the fund if allocated towards a bad investment as it can also amplify losses within. However, the fund consists of investments that have average maturity of 4.27 years.

PDT delivers a blended approach between different asset classes that can deliver both consistent income and capital appreciation. According to the most recent annual report, the current breakdown is as follows:

  • Common Stocks: 44.2%.
  • Corporate Bonds: 28.9%.
  • Preferred Securities: 26.1%.
  • Capital Preferred Securities: 0.7%.

The fund is most heavily concentrated within Financial and Utilities investments, accounting for 42.42% and 37.51% of investments respectively. Therefore, we can assume that these two sectors will have the greatest impact on PDT's daily price changes and overall total return potential. Digging deeper, the majority of the investments within PDT are allocated towards various banks and electric utility companies.

From a top holding perspective, we can see that Kinder Morgan, Inc. (KMI) is the largest holding at 3.95% of assets. This is followed by weight in AT&T Inc. (T) and Verizon Communications Inc. (VZ), accounting for 3.83% and 3.57% of assets respectively. The top ten holdings aren't companies that are generally known for their consistent or rapid earnings growth. Instead, these businesses are generally known for their consistent cash generation year after year, which is exactly what PDT is going for. Here are some notable achievements of the top holdings:

  • Verizon Communications Inc. (VZ): Over 20 years of consecutive dividend raises without disruption.
  • Public Service Enterprise Group Incorporated (PEG): Maintained a 5.04% dividend CAGR over the last five years.
  • BP p.l.c. (BP): Higher starting dividend yield of 5.5% with a very safe dividend payout ratio of 9.65%.

Updated Valuation

Since PDT operates as a closed-end fund, the price can trade at a difference to the actual value of underlying net assets. As a result, the price currently trades at a discount to NAV of approximately 5.3%. At the time of my last coverage, the price traded at a smaller discount to NAV of about 3.8%. For reference, PDT has traded at an average discount to NAV of only 2.1% over the last three-year period. Therefore, entry here is attractive since we have the opportunity to accumulate shares at a lower discount to NAV than usual. Looking over the ten-year period, there was also a time when PDT consistently traded at a premium to NAV. If market conditions improve, I believe it's possible for the price to reach the premium territory again.

The latest annual report also reinforces that PDT trades in a buy zone. For instance, net investment income landed at $0.52 per share while net realized gains amounted to $3.48 per share for fiscal year 2024. This resulted in a net increase from investment operations of $4.00 per share, which fully supported the annual distribution and ultimately led to the NAV growing back above its pandemic levels. For reference, here are the year-ending NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $12.84.
  • 2021: $14.88.
  • 2022: $12.60.
  • 2023: $10.58.
  • 2024: $13.59.

My original thesis was that interest rate cuts would serve as a positive catalyst for the growth of the underlying assets within PDT. Since the portfolio is primarily concentrated within Financials and Utilities, higher interest rates would have made it more difficult for these assets to grow their valuations. However, both of these sectors have experienced upward momentum despite interest rates remaining elevated.

Due to elevated inflation levels, it looks like the Fed has now adjusted its outlook. The Fed estimates there to only be one interest rate cut to take place over 2025. Therefore, we can expect a 'higher for longer' interest rate environment. Equities generally grow at a slower pace during times when interest rates are elevated because the higher cost of debt can delay any growth initiatives that businesses have planned.

Risk Profile

My main concern when it comes to slower growth is the fund's ability to continue growing its NAV and fully support the annual distribution amount. Referencing back to the same annual report, net investment income levels have been inconsistent and have decreased from their pandemic levels. As a result, the fund has revealed itself to be reliant on net realized gains to fuel underlying NAV growth and cover the distributions.

This reliance on net realized gains has made the fund vulnerable to market downturns. As you can imagine, it may be difficult and very unlikely for the fund to be able to generate positive net realized gains when market indexes are declining. For instance, PDT wasn't that much more resilient than the SPDR® S&P 500® ETF Trust (SPY) during 2022's market decline. Market indexes declined because of a Fed rate shock, as the Fed aggressively hiked interest rates to their decade highs. Despite having exposure to preferred securities and common stock in sectors that are generally considered to be more stable, PDT still reported losses despite its higher dividend yield.

PDT experienced a total loss of $1.11 per share for 2022, which wasn't enough to cover the annual distribution amount of $1.17 per share. As a result, these losses slightly amplified the NAV deterioration experienced over 2022. Going forward, I think PDT would be better aligned to navigate market downturns if management actively worked to improve the net investment income generated from underlying holdings. This would make PDT's price a lot more stable over time and reduce the reliance on positive market conditions to drive growth.

Dividend Sustainability

As of the most recently declared monthly distribution of $0.0825 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7.5%. If we multiply this monthly payout by twelve, we get an estimated annual payout of $0.99 per share, assuming that the distribution amount doesn't change. Based on the fund's performance history, it is certainly possible for PDT to sustain the current distribution rate going forward. Looking back over the fund's payout history, we can see that distributions were slightly reduced in mid-2023 to offset the losses experienced in the year prior. The dividend cut has clearly helped the fund regain its positive momentum and now the NAV has increased substantially.

At the current payout amount, I don't see a reason for the fund to reduce the distribution any further. Depending on performance over the course of 2025, there's even a chance that investors receive a dividend raise. If NAV growth also increases over 2025, I believe this is a very likely outcome. Therefore, PDT is a solid choice for investors looking to create a sizeable income stream over a long time horizon. Despite the slight reduction, PDT's payouts have remained in a consistent range. This consistency means that investors can essentially create their own dividend growth by reinvesting portions of all distributions that are paid out.

It's worth noting that the distributions seem to be mostly comprised of net investment income, which indicates that the distributions are likely classified as ordinary dividends. Ordinary dividends have less favorable tax consequences compared to the qualified dividends that you'd receive from more traditional dividend-paying stocks. Based on your specific tax bracket, desired income level, and amount of capital invested, PDT may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account to offset any potential tax burdens.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on PDT as I believe the valuation is attractive and there can be more upside to take place. The price currently sits at a larger discount to NAV than usual and the NAV has increased year over year. The fund was able to capture a sizeable amount of net realized gains which ultimately led to the NAV growing above its pandemic levels. The positive shift in momentum has proven that the fund can still excel in a higher interest rate environment. Distributions are now fully supported by the fund's earnings and there is enough excess coverage to alleviate any concerns of a dividend cut in the near future.

data.title: TCW Relative Value Mid Cap Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Environment

Indices in the U.S. and Europe hit records amid optimism over central bank easing, declining inflation, increasing optimism for a soft landing, and China stimulus. For the first time since 2020, the Federal Reserve (the Fed) cut interest rates doing so by 50 bps. After making its first cut (25 bps) since emerging from Covid on August 1, the Bank of England opted to keep rates steady at its latest meeting. The Bank of Japan also stayed put with its rate “around 0.25%.” The BoJ is the one major central bank in a hiking mode however is preferring to stay put until the new government is in place. To jumpstart its struggling economy, the Peoples Bank of China announced a broad stimulative package to combat slowing growth and waning investor confidence. Steps taken include cutting rates and incentivizing stock purchases and bank loans. In addition, the country announced a surprising fiscal stimulus package. These moves are major pivots from prior calls for “patience” and were reflected with strong gains in the Shanghai Composite.

U.S. job growth continues but at a slower pace. The August jobs numbers were below estimates and there were downward revisions to the prior months. The unemployment rate slightly dipped to 4.2%. Productivity was up 2% and the employment cost index softened, which should be supportive for corporate earnings. Wages are still higher than inflation and lower automobile gasoline prices are benefiting consumers. Recent U.S. economic releases (Empire and Philly Fed indexes, retail sales, industrial production, housing, and unemployment claims) were better than expected and are not recessionary indicators.

U.S. single-family housing starts spiked by 15.8% in August while permits were up 2.8%. Aided by lower mortgage rates, mortgage applications and refinance activity have risen noticeably. The Atlanta Fed GDPNow estimate for Q3 growth is ~ 3%.

With Q2 2024 earnings season complete, per UBS†, S&P 500 revenues grew over 5% YoY and earnings were up over 11% including stock buybacks. Big 6 Tech+ was the main force in Q2 up nearly 40% YoY but the group is expected to decelerate to just 21% in Q4 while the rest of the S&P 500 earnings growth is picking up. Earnings growth for both Financials and Utilities sectors were up double digits (+13%). Nvidia’s† Q2 earnings report disappointed even with a “beat and raise” as earnings fell short of analysts’ elevated expectations.

Sector Weightings1

For the third quarter, all sectors in the Russell Midcap Value were in the positive save for energy. The best performers were utilities (+18.0%), real estate (+16.0%), and financials (+12.6%) with industrials (+11.4%) and consumer discretionary (+11.1%) not too far behind. After energy (-2.6%), the bottom sectors were IT (+4.7%), consumer staples (+5.3%), and materials (+6.8%). Energy stocks fell in tandem with the commodity on hopes of reduced Middle East tensions though they may be tested with the recent increased hostilities. The portfolio’s sector weights detracted slightly with the benefit of being overweight financials and underweight consumer staples outweighed by the underweights in utilities and real estate and overweight in energy.

Security Selection1

Usually a positive contributor, stock selection detracted in the quarter. The portfolio’s top ten average-weighted names returned 15.0%, on average, better than the portfolio and benchmark index led by robust gains from Toll Brothers (TOL; 3.91%**) and Jones Lang LaSalle (JLL; 3.73%**), each with 30%+ returns. The portfolio achieved its best contribution from its real estate names which gained 21.7% outpacing the group return of 16.0% led largely by Jones Lang LaSalle and secondarily Welltower (WELL; 2.30%**). Homebuilder Toll Brothers and secondarily its peer Lennar (LEN; 1.39%**) highlighted in consumer discretionary where the portfolio’s stocks outperformed 13.9% versus 11.1%. The portfolio also benefited from its IT names.

On the downside, the portfolio’s industrials names returned 6.3% behind the group’s 11.4% rise with FedEx (FDX; 2.45%**) and Manitowoc (MTW; 0.19%**) largely responsible. Equitable Holdings (EQH; 3.03%**) was mostly to blame for the underperformance in financials where the portfolio’s stocks fell short 10.7% versus 12.6%. Acadia Healthcare (ACHC; 1.03%**) was the main culprit in health care. The company’s stock fell after a government probe into patient care. As a result of a fundamental review, the position was reduced by one-half.

Representative Buys (Ticker, Sector)*

Omnicom Group Inc. (OMC; Communication Services; 0.65%**)

MSCI ESG2 Score: Overall BBB

Omnicom Group Inc., headquartered New York, NY, is a leading provider of data-inspired, creative marketing and sales solutions. Omnicom’s iconic agency brands are home to the industry’s most innovative communications specialists which are focused on driving intelligent business outcomes for their clients. The company offers a wide range of services in advertising, strategic media planning, buying, precision marketing, retail and digital commerce, branding, experiential, public relations, healthcare marketing, and other specialty marketing services to over 5,000 clients in more than 70 countries. At initiation OMC had a market capitalization of $20.6 billion and met four of the five valuation factors: price-to-sales, price-to-cash-flow, price-to-earnings, and dividend yield of 2.8%. The fundamental catalysts are new products/new markets as the company continue to offer new creative solutions while at the same time expand its geographical footprint.

Teva Pharmaceutical (TEVA; Health Care; 0.91%**)

MSCI ESG Score: Overall BB

Teva Pharmaceutical Industries Ltd., headquartered in Tel Aviv, Israel, with its U.S. headquarters in Parsippany, NJ, is engaged in the development, production, and sale of pharmaceuticals. The company is focused on generics, innovative proprietary products, and over the counter (OTC) medicine. At initiation Teva had capitalization of $17.1 billion and met four of the five valuation factors: price-to-sales, price-to-cash flow, price-to-earnings, and price-to-book. The fundamental catalysts are new management and new products/markets. Richard Francis joined Teva in January 2023 as President and Chief Executive Officer. With over 25 years of experience, his resume includes management positions at Novartis†, Sandoz AG (CEO), and most recently CEO of Purespring Therapeutics. He is committed to refocusing the company on new biosimilar products and building the TEVA pipeline of innovative products.

Western Alliance Bancorp (WAL; Financials; 1.53%**)

MSCI ESG Score: Overall BBB

Western Alliance Bancorp, headquartered in Phoenix, AZ, is a commercial and retail bank with 37 branches in six states with significant presence in faster growing states, Arizona, California, and Nevada. At initiation, the stock had a $7.4 billion market capitalization and met four of the five valuation factors: price-to-sales, price-to-earnings, price-to-book, and higher than market yield. The primary catalyst is restructuring as management has been making advanced preparations to become a Category IV bank years ahead of piercing the crucial $100 billion in asset threshold. The shares have yet to fully recover from the failure of SVB Financial, Signature Bank, and First Republic Bank in early 2023, and shares sold off again in sympathy when another bank was ill-prepared to be regulated as a Category IV bank; this bank took no preparatory action, and the regulators stepped in to force the bank to cut its dividend, raise capital, increase its reserves, and reduce loan concentration. By contrast, WAL’s management team had the foresight to hire consultants with regulatory experience, raised the floor of internal capital requirement to Category IV average, and purposefully slowed growth during the transition. Western Alliance ended the first quarter of 2024 with $77 billion in assets, but much of the requisite expenses have already been taken and we expect the transition to Category IV to be fairly seamless. It has already reached its capital floor of 11% CET1, with a broadly diversified loan book. WAL does have a below Category IV average reserve ratio of 0.74%, but appear justified against non-performing assets/total assets of 0.53% and net charge-offs/loans of just 0.08%. During the 2023 bank failures, which were in part triggered by massive deposit runs, Western Alliance’s management has been so transparent and communicative with its largest deposit customers, it has witnessed steady deposit growth, even during bouts of volatility. In the past year ended first quarter, loans and deposits grew by 9.2% and 30.8% respectively. We believe the company is now well positioned and poised for notable stock price improvement over the next 1-2 year time horizon.

Representative Sells (Ticker Sector)†*

Carlisle Companies (CSL; Industrials) MSCI ESG Score: Overall A

Carlisle Companies, headquartered in Scottsdale, AZ, is the leading U.S. supplier of premium roofing materials primarily serving the non-residential market as well as weatherproofing products and insulation for the residential and non-residential end markets. Over the approximately three-and-a-half year investment, the management team divested its non-core braking, fluids, and medical/aerospace interconnect businesses while expanding its wallet share of a new building facility by acquiring a company engaged in weatherproofing and sealants. These portfolio actions along with its lean manufacturing prowess helped CSL achieve its Vision 2025 EPS goal earlier than expected. Recently, CSL leadership formulated a new five-year plan to grow earnings at a 15% annual rate through 2030. As investors appreciated the company’s renewed focus as a pure-play construction products company as well as its strong execution against its five-year Vision 2025 plan, the stock appreciated to the point of meeting none of the five valuation factors, thus prompting an automatic elimination of the position.

Dollar Tree, Inc. (DLTR; Consumer Discretionary)

MSCIESGScore:OverallAAA(Environmental:AA,Social:BBB,Governance:AA)

Dollar Tree, founded in 1986 and headquartered in Chesapeake, VA, is a leading operator of discount variety stores. The company operates 8,415 stores under the Dollar Tree banner which offers merchandise predominantly at the opening price point of $1.25, and Family Dollar which operates 8,359 general merchandise discount stores at multiple price points. At elimination, the stock had a $14.6 billion market capitalization and met four of the five valuation factors as it does not pay a dividend. Catalysts for the investment were new management, restructuring, and new products/new markets. Former Dollar General† CEO Rick Dreiling joined Dollar Tree’s Board of Directors as Executive Chairman in March 2022 and was appointed to the role of Chief Executive Officer in January 2023. At Dollar Tree, management has expanded its multi-price product assortment to 1,600 stores to date. The company introduced $3 and $5 Dollar Tree Plus product in select discretionary categories and later expanded to $3, $4 and $5 frozen and refrigerated products in order to increase customer traffic and store productivity. In Q4 2023, management initiated a comprehensive store portfolio optimization review and identified approximately 970 underperforming Family Dollar stores, including 600 stores to be closed in the first half of fiscal 2024 and 370 stores to be closed at the end of each store’s lease term. Thus far, the company has closed approximately 655 stores and expects to close an additional 45 during the remainder of the year. In Q2 2024, it initiated a formal review of strategic alternatives for the Family Dollar business segment which could include a potential sale, spin-off, or other disposition of the business. The news initially brought optimism as the banner’s performance has created a consistent overhang to the stock price performance. However, the company’s inability to provide any visibility on its likelihood to successfully dispose of Family Dollar during the last earnings call, on the top of a deteriorating performance from the Dollar Tree banner due to a more constrained environment for the lower-end consumer and intensifying competitive pressures, led us to exit the position.

Evercore Partners (EVR; Financials)

MSCI ESG Score: Overall BB

Evercore Partners, based in New York, NY is an investment banking advisory and investment management company. At initiation, Evercore had a market capitalization of $1.8 billion and met two out of the five valuation criteria, price-to-earnings and dividend yield. The primary catalyst was new markets as the company augments its investment banking and advisory services for multinational corporations and private equity firms on large and complex transactions. Despite being on the cusp of a new mergers & acquisition cycle, the shares have appreciated to a point that it no longer meets any of the valuation factors, signifying it is no longer a value stock, as we define it. Consistent with our self-imposed rules, the stock was sold.

Market Outlook

While there are some cracks, the U.S. economy remains resilient and a standout among developed markets, and recession odds are at normal historical levels of 20-30%. With the Fed entering a rate cutting cycle, sentiment among buyers and homebuilders is increasing; mortgage rates have declined, mortgage applications have risen, and refinancing activity has gained momentum. Home Depot† has mentioned in calls that it typically experiences a pick-up in pro activity within two quarters after the Fed begins to cut rates. Alleviating concerns, at least in the near term, Congress passed a short-term funding bill which will keep the government funded through December 20. One near-term headwind has been forestalled with a temporary agreement ending the International Longshoremen’s Association (ILA) three-day strike that began October 1. Including the 33,000+ Boeing† machinists that are still on strike since September 13, the October payrolls number will be affected.

Inflation continues to cool across the globe. The Fed’s preferred inflation gauge (Core PCE) has fallen to 2.2% annualized for August. Singapore’s CPI in August fell to 2.2% YoY (from a high of 3.4% in February), Australia’s declined to 2.7% YoY, and though still somewhat elevated, Brazil’s annual rate of inflation slowed much more than expected with prices up 4.2%versus estimates of 4.3%.

German business confidence (IFO) declined for a fourth consecutive month in September and by more than expected adding to signs that the eurozone’s largest economy may have tipped into recession. “The German economy is coming under increasing pressure,” said IFO president Clemens Fuest while IFO economist Klaus Wohlrabe stated, “The German economy is on the edge of a downward spiral.”

Prices follow earnings. S&P 500 company earnings ex. Big 6 are poised to accelerate high single to low double digits into year-end while those mega cap names are expected to decelerate. Slower growth but no hard landing will be supportive for companies not already buoyed by AI infrastructure. Lower gas prices are a tailwind for moderate consumer spending and wages are still outpacing inflation meaning real purchasing power. Fiscal stimulus (2022 Inflation Reduction, 2021 Infrastructure Investment and Jobs, and 2023 Chips Acts) monies continue and are not expected to peak until 2026-2027, bolstering future earnings.

Fourth quarter is historically the strongest seasonally, especially in election years. The S&P 500 tends to pull back/consolidate in the fall but then resume its run into year end. Uncertainty about the upcoming presidential election (with polls suggesting a statistical tie) is creating some volatility, but in any given year the U.S. stock market experiences 3-4 5-10% corrections. Geopolitical risks abound (especially the heightened hostilities in the Middle East) which are likely to result in higher oil prices, but we believe the U.S. is a global haven for all asset classes. Our base case is for the U.S. equity markets to go up which historically occurs 70% of the time and rarely down in an election year no matter which party is in control. Even so, all portfolio holdings are stress tested for excessive leverage in case of a recession over the next two years and we completed a tariff macro stress test in the event of a Trump victory in November. Blackrock† estimates $6.5 trillion of cash on sidelines and $2 trillion in CDs which is likely to move as the Fed continues its rate cutting cycle.

Investors are likely to remain in a holding pattern until the election has been determined at which point they begin to position portfolios for the new administration. We are busy and actively working to make the best portfolio decisions possible with the information we have and will adjust should the “facts” change. We are constructive about the future but maintain strong risk controls for unexpected turbulent events.

Diane E. Jaffee, CFA, Group Managing Director

Mona E. Eraiba, Senior Vice President

Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Investment Risks

Equity investments entail equity risk and price volatility risk. Funds investing in mid and small cap companies involve special risks including higher volatility and lower liquidity. Please see the Fund’s Prospectus for more information on these and other risks.

MSCI ESG Ratings

An MSCI ESG Rating is designed to measure a company’s resilience to long-term, industry material environmental, social and governance (ESG) risks. MSCI uses a rules-based methodology to identify industry leaders and laggards according to their exposure to ESG risks and how well they manage those risks relative to peers. MSCI’s ESG Ratings range from leader (AAA, AA), average (A, BBB, BB) to laggard (B, CCC). Leader: A company leading its industry in managing the most significant ESG risks and opportunities; Average: A company with a mixed or unexceptional track record of managing the most significant ESG risks and opportunities relative to industry peers; Laggard: A company lagging its industry based on its high exposure and failure to manage significant ESG risks. ESG risks and opportunities can vary by industry and company. The MSCI ESG Ratings model identifies the ESG risks (called Key Issues), that are most material to a GICS® sub-industry or sector.

To learn more, please visit www.msci.com/esg-investing. Certain information © 2021 MSCI ESG Research, LLC. Reproduced by permission.

Glossary of Terms

Bank Loans – An amount of money loaned at interest by a bank to a borrower, usually on collateral security, for a certain period of time. Base Case – A term used to describe the highest probability outcome in the opinion of the presenter or author. It is often used in conjunction with describing other possible outcomes usually labeled “downside”, “negative”, “adverse”, “upside”, or “positive”. Basis Points (bps) – A unit that is equal to 1/100th of 1%, and is used to denote the change in a financial instrument. The basis point is commonly used for calculating changes in interest rates, equity indexes and the yield of a fixed-income security. Benchmark– A standard against which the performance of a security, mutual fund or investment manager can be measured. Generally, broad market and market-segment stock and bond indexes are used for this purpose. BOE (Bank of England) – United Kingdom central bank. BOJ (Bank of Japan) – Japanese central bank. Buybacks– The repurchase of outstanding shares by a company in order to reduce the number of shares on the market. Cash and Equivalents – Assets that include but are not limited to Discount Notes, Commercial Paper, Money Market Funds, STIFs, Certificates of Deposit, T-bills, Tri-Party Repo, U.S. Treasury and Agency Notes within 13 months to maturity, and cash. While management generally believes that most, possibly all, of the cash and equivalents would be available on a same day or next day basis under stable market conditions, we cannot predict the impact of any adverse developments in market conditions that might occur. Cash Flow – The movement of money into or out of a business, project, or financial product. CCC Rating – A CCC rating represents an extremely high risk bond or investment; banks are not allowed to invest in CCC rated bonds. CCC bonds are junk bonds. CD (Certificates of Deposit) – An FDIC-insured savings certificate issued by a bank to a depositor for a specified length of time. The bank pays a predetermined interest rate to the depositor for the duration of the CD. Central Bank – A monopolized and often nationalized institution given privileged control over the production and distribution of money and credit. Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1) – A component of Tier 1 capital that consists mostly of common stock held by a bank or other financial institution. Consumer Price Index (CPI) – Measures the average change in prices over time that consumers pay for a basket of goods and services. Core PCE Index – Measures the prices paid by consumers for goods and services without the volatility caused by movements in food and energy prices to reveal underlying inflation trends. Corporate – Of or relating to a bond issued by a corporation as opposed to a bond issued by the U.S. Treasury, a non-U.S. government or a municipality. Dividend Yield – A financial ratio that shows how much a company pays out in dividends each year relative to its share price. In the absence of any capital gains, the dividend yield is the return on investment for a stock. Developed Markets – Countries that have sound, well-established economies and are therefore thought to offer safer, more stable investment opportunities than developing markets. Dividend – A distribution of a portion of a company’s earnings, decided by the board of directors, to a class of its shareholders. Easing – A monetary policy in which a central bank purchases government securities or other securities from the market in order to lower interest rates and increase the money supply. EPS (Earnings Per Share) – The portion of a company’s profit allocated to each outstanding share of common stock. ESG – Environmental, Social, and Governance. Eurozone – A geographic and economic region that consists of all the European Union countries that have fully incorporated the euro as their national currency. Expense Ratio – A measure of what it costs an investment company to operate a mutual fund. Federal Reserve (the Fed) – The central bank of the United States which regulates the U.S. monetary and financial system. GDP (Gross Domestic Product) – The market value of all final goods and services produced within a country in a given period of time. Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS)– A standardized classification system for equities developed jointly by Morgan Stanley Capital International (MSCI) and Standard & Poor’s. Growth– A diversified portfolio of stocks that has capital appreciation as its primary goal, with little or no dividend payouts. Portfolio companies would mainly consist of companies with above-average growth in earnings that reinvest their earnings into expansion, acquisitions, and/or research and development. Inflation– A condition of a rise in the general level of prices of goods and services in an economy over a period of time. Interest Rate – The proportion of a loan that is charged as interest to the borrower, typically expressed as an annual percentage of the loan outstanding. Leverage– The use of borrowed money to increase investing power. A firm with significantly more debt than equity is considered to be highly leveraged. Market Capitalization– Represents the aggregate value of a company or stock. It is obtained by multiplying the number of shares outstanding by their current price per share. Mergers And Acquisitions (M&A) – A general term used to refer to the consolidation of companies. A merger is a combination of two companies to form a new company, while an acquisition is the purchase of one company by another in which no new company is formed. Outperform– Outperform is when an investment is expected to perform better than the return generated by a particular index or the overall market. Since the performance of many investments is compared to a benchmark index, outperform refers to generating a higher return than a particular benchmark over time. Outperform also refers to an analyst’s rating on a security, and outperform is a better rating than neutral and worse than a strong buy recommendation. Overweight – A condition where the portfolio exposure to a given asset class (or risk measure) exceeds that of the benchmark index. Price-to-Book (P/BV or Price/Book or P/B) – A ratio used to compare a stock’s market value to its book value. It is calculated by dividing the current closing price of the stock by the latest quarter’s book value per share. Price-to-Cash Flow– A ratio that measures the market’s expectations of a firm’s future financial health. It is calculated by dividing the share price by the cash flow per share. Price-to-Earnings (P/E) – A valuation ratio of a company’s current share price compared to its per-share earnings. Price-to-Sales – A ratio for valuing a stock relative to its own past performance, other companies, or the market itself. Private Equity – An asset class consisting of equity securities and debt in operating companies that are not publicly traded on a stock exchange. Recession – Two consecutive quarters of negative economic growth as measured by a country’s gross domestic product. Reserve Requirement Ratio – A central bank regulation employed by most, but not all, of the world’s central banks, that sets the minimum fraction of customer deposits and notes that each commercial bank must hold as reserves (rather than lend out). Standard & Poor’s500Index(S&P500)– An index of 505 stocks issued by 500 large companies with market capitalizations of at least $6.1 billion. It is seen as a leading indicator of U.S. equities and a reflection of the performance of the large-cap universe. Stimulus – Something that causes a reaction; an incentive. Stress Test – A simulation technique used on asset and liability portfolios to determine their reactions to different financial situations. Stress tests are also used to gauge how certain stressors will affect a company or industry. They are usually computer-generated simulation models that test hypothetical scenarios. Underperform – When an investment is underperforming, it is not keeping pace with other securities or indices. Tariff – A tax imposed on imported goods and services. Technicals– Analyzing statistics generated by market activity, such as past prices and volume. Underweight– A condition where a portfolio does not hold a sufficient amount of a particular security when compared to the security’s weight in the underlying benchmark portfolio. Unemployment Rate– The percentage of the total labor force that is unemployed but actively seeking employment and willing to work. Valuations – The process of determining the current worth of an asset or company. There are many techniques that can be used to determine value, some are subjective and others are objective. Value – A fund that primarily holds stocks that are deemed to be undervalued in price and that are likely to pay dividends. Volatility – A measure of the risk of price moves for a security calculated from the standard deviation of day-to-day logarithmic historical price changes. Yield Curve – A line that plots the interest rates, at a set point in time, of bonds having equal credit quality, but differing maturity dates. The most frequently reported yield curve compares the three-month, two-year, five-year and 30-year U.S. Treasury debt. This yield curve is used as a benchmark for other debt in the market, such as mortgage rates or bank lending rates. The curve is also used to predict changes in economic output and growth. YTD – Year-to-date. YoY – Year-over-year.
